What do you think of when band members go solo? Do you think there is any way it can work out afterwards?
I have recently become intrigued by this topic. As a long time k-pop fan it was very typical for a band to have its members start solo careers, but typically towards the end of the life of the band (about 5 or 7 years, for luckier bands 10 years after debut). It was always an interesting process to see how the individual members would express themselves but it also could be a dumpster fire, especially for the group/band as whole and their future endeavors. This concept only returned to my mind after recently getting into Maneskin and subsequently learning that they are on a break...to pursue solo interests.

It's interesting isn't it? A break/hiatus, solo interests. In the west, a hiatus (one that is made official not just the band being quiet for a while) and the solo endeavors for a band typically spells out disaster for the band itself to put it lightly. Just look at 1 Direction, 5th Harmony and the endless list, but those weren't Italian rock bands but they do have something in common, being made famous by xfactor/music competition shows . In k-pop it is typically less tragic because it typically only occurs when the group has already or has started to fade out not at there peak or right after it (exceptions do exist), but here we have group in their prime taking a break. To me it usually means the following:

a) One member or more feels (and or management thinks) that they are fairly popular enough to pull off a "Beyoncé" (become successful beyond the group on there own).

b) there is some element of internal conflict making it hard for them to work together/priorities have shifted requiring them to take a break and maybe find something else to do in the meantime which will end up being solo work to help maintain relevance and or find themselves musically.

c) problems with whoever helps them release their music (e.g. record label) making it hard to do things together but maybe not necessarily on their won.

d) they are genuinely just taking a break for whatever reason but not due to internal conflict or a Beyoncé situation.

Regardless of the reason (but especially if it is a or b), this typically has a negative impact on the group and its music. The reunion is rarely as electric or to put it lightly like what the band was like before. Even in k-pop where it is basically scheduled ahead of the time for the lowest risk period of a band's lifespan, it typically spells itself out the same.

When they do comeback, it is often what I have come to call "reunion music", something made purely just to keep the fans at bay, maybe the contracual obligation comes to mind but in my opinion most bands do participate willingly and enthusiastically. It often feels like a watered down version of themselves with some nostalgia bait thrown in either on purpose or by default because maybe they had a certain music style that brings back the memories etc. This effect isn't really felt if the band remains active during the "solos" and still releases music as they release solo work on their usual schedule. I also find that if they seemingly take a hiatus without actually having to announce it, it is not as severe, perhaps because things weren't so bad that they had to announce a hiatus.

While I do think it a basic right for a member of a band to have a solo career, I also think that it is hard to balance both a band obligation and a solo career. I think part of the reason the reunion release tends to suck is because priorities and interests of the band members have shifted. It is not rare that a member's own work will be wildly different from the main band's work and trying to apply that back to the band can be difficult. I also think if they don't have a clear vision for the band, then things will naturally fall apart when they return. Perhaps as a member does solo work they suddenly realise they don't want to do what they band used to do anymore as that's not how they want to express themselves in the moment. I find that for bands (k-pop) with both successful soloists and band careers, there is a certain level of compromise/sacrifice happening on both ends.

A soloist in a band has to manage both the creative work required for the band and their solo careers like 2 different projects at the same time which obviously limits the commitment they can put into their own work which often seems to limit their solo success. Some examples I would give would be Girl's Generations (GG/SNSD) Taeyeon when she intially first went solo. She was huge like mega huge but she seemed to tone it down a little (or her management did) to not overshadow GG all together since they still had plans together, Taemin from Shinee comes to mind too. If they wanted they could have easily just left their bands behind. A funny example of a member being simultaneously famous as a soloist and in big band was Gdragon (a literal icon of the industry) when he was in BigBang and I have no idea how they made that work but they did operate simultaneously. He was bigger than the band but the band still remained popular, infact more popular than other bands operating at the time. There are also plenty of examples of this going wrong with members of certain bands leaving or being heavily prioritised because their solo careers took off and a ghost band being left behind.

Enough of the k-pop, I am just curious if you think it is a realistic dynamic for a band to hand both responsibilities? I know it doesn't have to be but it feels like by the time a group is going solo, it means the band is about to die. That's why I am kind of concerned abour Maneskin's situation with fans getting excited to even see the members in the same place because it seems like a rare occurrence now. I am bummed out because I liked how audacious they were and they kind of gave me hope (for some reason, it's not like they make uplifiting music) perhaps maybe that's what the (my) world needs right now. Of course they have no obligation to serve my interests, I am jusy curious as to if my brain is making this pattern up.

IMO and experience with acts I like/follow, the only time it really spells disaster is if some/all of the other members can't write and have no real substance/creative curiosity without whoever goes solo.

Dave Matthews put out a great solo album in 2003 which was, at this point, 1/3rd of the way through their career and it had no impact on the band. Plus he's always done acoustic tours with just Tim Reynolds, albeit the same DMB songs, not necessarily a totally new project.

All the guys from Phish have solo projects, and not only hasn't that affected anything, they work a lot of their solo songs into their full band shows. They all have the utmost respect for each other.

Jim James has put out a number of solo records, My Morning Jacket hasn't been affected.

Marcus Mumford put a solo record out last year or so, still tours with the band and releases music with them.

All the Animal Collective guys have solo projects, they still make music together as well.

I could probably list a bunch more but these are off the top of my head. So yea, I think it only spells disaster if other member(s) of the band get a huge chip on their shoulder, for whatever reason. Otherwise, don't think it's an issue to let your bandmates explore a bit.

EDIT: I don't know the specifics of his leaving, but Father John Misty leaving Fleet Foxes could possibly be an example of someone leaving and staying out because of the success of their solo career. But, regardless, to the OP's point, Fleet Foxes still alive and well with or without him.

70s/80s prog is where the big solo beasts used to live.

Everyone in Genesis had a successful solo career. Phil Collins and Peter Gabriel, obviously. Steve Hackett and Mike Rutherford started spin-off bands/careers. Tony Banks tried in the 80s, failed completely, eventually started recording orchestral music in the 00s, and has done relatively well in that tiny niche.

In Pink Floyd, Gilmour, Waters, and Mason, all have viable side careers but make most of their money from playing various eras of Floyd.

Yes had Rick Wakeman and Jon Anderson running side careers, but never quite at headliner scale.

King Crimson's Fripp basically is King Crimson, but he has always had his own projects.

Prog never really had the "front person and everyone else is the backing band" dynamic that pop bands do, so there was always more scope for individual careers.

Lots of artists have done solo and side projects without it destroying the band. Maynard of Tool being a prime example as on the side he has A Perfect Circle and Puscifer. Yet Tool still exists still tours, and occasionally puts out new music. I think it just comes down to the egos of those involved. If one person believes they are the band and everyone else is just hired guns then it will break the band but in a band like Tool everyone contributes and no one puts themself above the others.

I think of Roger Waters. His importance in Pink Floyd was more of being the "idea man" than being the bass player. But Pink Floyd was a band whose musical talent contributed heavily to the conceptual ideas of Waters'. Beginning with Waters'first solo album, "The Wall" 🤣, the musicality of the band seemed to fade immensely. I think most fans would agree that Floyd was significantly more listenavle when all members played a role. I roll my eyes now trying to listen to his solo projects. Especially "Dark Side of the Moon Redux" which seemed unnecessary, narcissistic and a big FU to the rest of the band.

In the specific case of rock bands, whether they stay together depends on two things: love and money. Love, in the sense of where the band members feel most fulfilled, in the band or as solo artists; and money, in the sense of which path is more lucrative. There are plenty of cases where band members hate each other, break up, realize they're no longer earning enough to afford the private jet, and then get back together because they love the money more than they hate their bandmates. There are also cases where one of the artists is so successful solo that there's no point in returning to the band.

But I'll also use Paul McCartney with Let It Be as an example. Even though he had all the ingredients to shine as a solo artist (which he did), he fought to keep The Beatles together even though the other members were getting tired of it.

It's also normal and even healthy for band members to have side projects outside of the band. Simply because most members of a more or less famous band have already played in other bands and have musician friends. But these projects almost always have a lower profile.

I think when there's a musical direction that one member wants to go into that doesn't match the vibe of the band as a whole, a side-project or a solo run before getting back together can make a lot of sense.

Some examples:

Thom Yorke of Radiohead has had many successful, critically acclaimed projects in between Radiohead albums.

Someone else already mentioned Maynard from Tool, and he's a great example

John K. Sampson of Propaghandi (great, seminal 90's punk band) realized a bunch of his songs weren't really a fit for the hardcore political punk of Propaghandi and put out 4 amazing albums as The Weakerthans. I seriously love both bands, but Reconstruction Site by The Weakerthans is a life-changing album.

Dorthia Cottrell and Mike Scheidt both front some of the biggest doom metal bands of the past 25 years (Windhand and Yob, respectively), and both also put out phenomenal dark, psychedelic, acoustic folk albums.

New Edition had almost the whole group go and do solo work and almost all of it is pretty good. Bobby Brown was of course probably the most famous/successful one off that but even then Bel Biv Devoe also ended up being a pretty successful group too. Tresvant had a good body of solo work as well. So did Johnny Gill. When the group is filled with talented artists they can make it work even if they go solo.

Feel like it was pretty common back in the day with R&B groups since, again, most of them were pretty talented in their own right. The Revolution (Prince's band) had Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis two of the greatest living songwriters on earth who went on to make hits with the before mentioned New Edition, Janet Jackson and many many more.

With labels like Staxx/Motown always looking to put out new work there was an easy avenue for artists to venture out and try things on their own if they were already signed as part of a group.

Kevin Russell of The Gourds left for his own Shinyribs project. Worked out very well for him as he created his own identity and fan base. Now they do annual Gourds reunion shows and easily sell out theaters for a few hundred bucks a head tickets, so not a bad deal for the others as well.

I'm a Maneskin fan - started following them closely during their last tour. As others have pointed out, many examples of band members with (multiple) side projects coming back to the main band. Although they had a big reunion in Rome last month and a radio host announced a reunion at SanRemo (the host talked about it 2 days in a row!), sadly, I'm not hopeful we'll get new music any time soon.

There's been talk of Damiano going solo since X-factor so not a real shock. But to do it right when the band peaked internationally is too risky and made no sense. I assume the band imploded under the pressure and as a bid to talk some confidence back into Damiano, the label pitched the solo album.

The early roll out of his solo album focused on his mental health -- the stress, non-stop touring, being away from home. But that made no sense-- he didn't take a break -- instead he was writing/recording in LA, crossing the globe for promotion, then went on tour for 3 solid months. He admitted he wasn't in therapy at the time. He made a big deal about moving to LA (new home in a high rise even though he's deathly afraid of heights, new friends) and in interviews it was pretty clear he had negative reflections on his time in the band. During every tour date, he made a speech to this as well, some nights more negative than others (he was "stuck in it"). The way he talked about the band was a stark contrast to when Ethan, Vic, or Thomas were asked about their status. They were clearly enthusiastic about the band coming back together.

I don't think his solo album went the way the label had hoped. To me, he looked over it half way through the tour (looked bored and miserable, chewing gum instead of singing). It's been six months and no sign that the band is working on new music. It would not shock me if Damiano's currently working on solo album #2.

PS: Check out Thomas Raggi's project if you haven't. It's really good. I'm so impressed!

I've started to think they might be in the same limbo as Halsey or Dove Cameron. An artist who at first glance seems successful, but in reality, their last project didn't perform as the record label expected, so there's no funding to make another album or promote it, but they're not allowed to do anything else either.

So the whole May meeting is to reinforce the idea that they're still relevant. I also feel like it has a sort of marketing calendar, but I don't think we'll have anything significant planned for 2026. I hope I'm wrong.

For me, it's the original evokes the feeling of silence, and the tonal changes in it capture both how silence and darkness can be both profoundly intimate or vast and awe-inspiring, and it explores the tension between the overwhelming noise of human knowledge and the monastic peace of nothingness. It's not just the lyrics, it's how the music is composed.

The Disturbed version just doesn't get anything about what makes the song special, clearly only focusing only on the alienation of the 'neon god they made' part, almost certainly to make some edgelord 'the government is 1984-ing us' point, while singing it with zero interest in the musical or thematic ideas in the original, just giving it his biggest constipated-est anti-silence bellow.

A bad cover is forgettable, but a cover that actively undermines the original is the worst.

As someone who genuinely likes AJR, why do people hate it?
I'm back for the apparently yearly post about AJR. However, I want to change things up a little bit, since most of the time it's usually AJR "haters" talking to themselves. Fair warning, I'm not saying that your opinion is wrong or anything like that, I just want to see people correcting someone who likes their songs instead of the previous stuff.

Some of their songs and lyrics have pretty childish expressions mixed with a somewhat deep plot-point. We got "God is Really Real" where they talk about the band's Dad being terminally ill and how they'll distract the doctors and sneak him out; or "Don't Throw Out My Legos" where it's all about wanting to not leave your childhood behind. Then, they can also just have really stupid lyrics and reflections like in "The Maybe Man" where he wants to become a stone, a dog and god to get rid of his problems.

Yet somehow, I really vibe with their lyrics, it gives an honest, sympathetic and desperate view of their situation, even if it's not the biggest problem you've ever seen. Like sure, "growing up" is not the worst thing and boo-hoo for complaining about it when some people can't even pay rent next month. But...it's still deep, it's good to listen to it when you're not feeling down but are not upbeat enough for something more active. I like the fact that they see everything like a child to make the problem feel more pure, and they can also add random shit to make it seem the singer's brain is swaying from the main point ("3 O'Clock Things" and "World's Smallest Violin"), which makes it kind of relatable, even if it ruins the song, like as if you're having a conversation with some ADHD guy and it's like a fun chat.

I'd also like to mention the songs where they're just intrusive thoughts the singers had ("The Dumb Song" and "The Plane That Never Lands") or just funny conversations that the singer looks like they're having ("Karma").

This is not a "convince me I'm wrong" or "this is why you should try it out" post, I'm just curious how you'd debate about it with someone who likes their songs

EDIT: Thanks everyone for the responses and for taking your time to write comments and replies (and for downvoting the post /jk). I personally don’t like shrugging off haters even if it’s due to different preferences, so it feels refreshing seeing other people’s perspectives about artists without people blurting out insults and personal jabs for giving an opinion, I’ve learnt more about what some people find appealing in songs, what differs AJR from other singers and I’ve learnt that my resistance to “bad music” is high, since I’ve found myself agreeing with some of the statements. Although, I can also say that people’s use of music and lyrics in their life also affects their preferences, which did kind of polarise opinions a bit.

well, i dont like them for two different reasons:

the music itself. it feels like they throw everything they've ever listened to at the wall and none of it really sticks, especially if they try to combine it (particular example: not fond of how bud like you transitions from an acoustic drinking song for the chorus to edm trap for the verses). even as someone who loves bands like ween or mr bungle or in general music that combines influences from disparate genres, ajr doesn't do it for me because, simply put, they're not good at it and their choices are usually baffling.

the lyrics. actually, unlike most people here, this is my lesser problem with them, not that that's saying much. im into some lyricists who aren't terribly poetic but still earnest enough to endear themselves to me. i feel like ajr tries a similar earnest approach, but it's not a very mature or engaging execution, feels like a troupe of theater kids who love disney and nothing else. that's really the best way i can describe it.

now, i will say, as people, ajr seem pretty nice. and if you like ajr, more power to you (my mom loves them). these are just personal grievances. i hardly hate them, ive listened to far worse and far more detestable music (blood on the dance floor, yuck!)

Full disclosure, when I was a teenager, I really liked The Click. Then Neotheater came out and I thought it was one of the worst things I had ever heard. My taste has evolved over time as I've listened to a wider variety of music and now I can no longer stand The Click, either, but I still hold that it's leagues above Neotheater.

I don't like the childish approach. If done intentionally for a few individual songs with matching subject matter it can work, but when that's all of their music, I just get the feeling that they only know how to make childish music. If every album you make is about "viewing adulthood and the struggles of growing up through the lens of childhood," then it comes cross as creatively stagnant. You're just doing the same thing over and over.

Their electronic, frenzied style comes across as discordant a lot of the time. "Three-Thirty" is perhaps their biggest offender out of the songs I've heard.

The few songs by them that I think are okay are "Sober Up," "Dear Winter," "Role Models," and the original "Turning Out." All of which are relatively straightforward and serious songs that aren't constantly trying to wink in your face with their craaaaaaaazy references.

Their worst, IMO, are "Birthday Party" (which is perhaps the single worst song I've ever heard in all my days on this earth), "Three-Thirty," "Beats," and "100 Bad Days." They have yet to master the art of a half-decent beat drop.

I don't think that unfiltered word-vomit lyrics, no matter how sincere the sentiments they carry, make for good art. I think some degree of refinement is necessary. I admire their commitment to emotional honesty in their music, but it works against them more than it works for them.

For reference, some of my favorite artists are The Smiths, Radiohead, Mitski, The Mountain Goats, Joyce Manor, and Bassvictim.

How close is Gothic Metal to Alt-Pop?
Hi all,

For the past few months I've been getting into Scandinavian Gothic Metal, both its roots as a progenitor of Symphonic Metal and its poppier end. Bands like Sentenced, Poisonblack, Paradise Lost, Theatre of Tragedy, To/Die/For, Entwine, and HIM have sent me down a rabbit hole of the genre's history and development.

One thing I've become curious about is whether there's a throughline between the poppier end of Gothic Metal and what would later be called dark alt-pop.

The question really came to mind when I discovered To/Die/For's habit of covering songs like The Cure's "Lovesong," Cutting Crew's "(I Just) Died in Your Arms," and even Paula Abdul's "Straight Up." I was surprised by how naturally those songs seemed to fit their sound.

Is there a musical lineage here, or am I just noticing superficial similarities? If there is a connection, where would you place bands like To/Die/For, Entwine, and HIM in relation to dark alt-pop and adjacent genres? And perhaps most importantly, would alt-pop fans find these bands appealing?

I'd be especially interested in hearing from people who listen to both scenes, since I'm much more familiar with metal than alt-pop.

Alt-pop is a terrible term because it doesn't relate to a specific sound, but rather non-mainstream sound in pop music. While some of the original influences of gothic metal could have been called at the time alt-pop, nowadays Lorde and Billie Eilish are called alt-pop, and, obviously, they have no connection to gothic metal.

Gothic metal is pretty much doom metal with gothic/death rock elements, so the link is direct.

By the way I remembered that ToDieFor made a cover of Maria Magdalena by Sandra (Lauer), which is originally a mainstream synthpop song. That fact doesn't make gothic metal closer to synthpop, rather shows that ToDieFor took elements from synthpop and apparently they love that music.

Gothic Metal is not a genre too. There is doom metal with synthesised orchestras and female singing, there is european brand which can be either harder version of 80s gothic rock or straight simple heavy metal with moody atmosohere and deep baritone vocals, there also are pop metal bands with female vocalist. All of this is described as Gothic Metal but those are at leas three different genres in terms of actual music. Paradise Lost is often described as Gothic Metal but they aren't, although they tried at least two of the versions of Gothic Metal at some points in their carreer.

Seldom do you see it the other way around: i.e. people disliking or not connecting with the early work but connecting with later works. It happens. Look at Chappell Roan or Twenty One Pilots.

Punk Genre music and what it entails
I tried to pose this question to the Punk subreddit but obviously they must get it a lot and it was removed by the moderation team. But this is something I really would love to dive into further and get possibly some band recommendations. I listen to bands that I would consider punk but I'm not sure if the vast majority of people would.

Bands such as:
Nirvana
Sum 41
Rise against
Linkin park (not the new singer, nothing against her I just don't like her voice as much as the original.)
Three days grace (less so now that they started using AI)
Tool
Lamb of God
The Narcissist Cookbook (Not rock but falls in well with the ideology.)

I love these bands and the messaging behind most of their music. But I want to broaden my horizons and properly narrow down what punk entails if that makes sense. I would love to talk to some people that have been more into the actual subculture over just the music. Because while I have been listening to Nirvana since I was a baby (I'm 28, which is why all these bands are old as hell) I never got too big into the subculture for personal issue sort of reasons.

I've been in and around punk scenes for a while, both online and in local scenes, and your question got removed from r/punk because "what even is punk?" is perhaps the most tired, repeatedly asked question in the scene. People are sick of answering it. Also, this sub has pretty strict rules against asking for recommendations, so I won't be surprised if this post gets removed.

But just for the sake of doing it one more time, here's the shortest primer I can possibly give you:

Punk is a subgenre of rock that started out as a reaction against the art rock of the 1970s. The original punk bands in New York City (The Ramones, etc.) consciously stripped rock down to its barest essentials: songs built around three-chord progressions played fast, loud and stupid.

A subculture grew around the music, with much of that subculture being rooted in do-it-yourself ideals (i.e. the band members themselves doing their own promo, booking and hosting their own shows and creating their own lables). The DIY ethos also took on an anti-corporate, pro-mutual aid ethos which gave punk the left-leaning or even radically leftist politics it's known for today.

Over time, the genre ended up being one of the most important in rock, and it's shifted and morphed as new bands bring new ideas. A lot of that, musically speaking, came down to basically just doing "punk + something else," which is why a band like Minor Threat can be described as hardcore punk while The Cure can be described as post-punk. Those bands don't sound anything alike, but they ultimately come from the same source.

The weird result of this is that now there are a lot of bands that have the punk sound but don't have the punk ethos, and there are a lot of bands that have the ethos that don't have the sound, and plenty that have both. I'd argue that Green Day is a good example of a band that had the sound more than it does now, but they've lost the ethos over time. Kinda hard to be anti-corporate while doing brand deals with fuckin 7/11. Some bands you mentioned, like Tool and Lamb of God, have the left-leaning politics, but they are basically not at all influenced by punk (Tool is alternative metal or progressive metal; Lamb of God is groove metal).

As a final note, part of the DIY ethos of punk is, like, learning to do a Google search and find bands yourself.

What you're stumbling into is the core of why "what even is punk?" has been such a constant, neverending debate for the past 50 years. It's an inherent problem with the fact that the word describes both a genre of music that has a very specific structure (three chords, loud, fast, played by amateurish musicians, etc.) AND a subculture with distinct elements that don't have anything to do with music, even though the subculture is built around this very specific genre of music.

Part of the problem here comes down to the fact that, like I said, the left-leaning politics and DIY ethos developed over time, rather than being there from the beginning. The Ramones weren't singing songs about ending war, they were singing songs about listening to rock n' roll while you were in high school.

Ultimately, the term is a bit of an "I know it when I see it" thing, and it depends on what we're talking about. Structurally speaking, Olivia Rodrigo's song "Good for You" IS a punk rock track. It does everything that punk is supposed to do from a MUSICAL perspective. But if you said that Olivia IS a punk herself, people would think you're nuts.

Some people think that the DIY, leftist ethos needs to be present in a song in order for it to be considered "punk." I don't think this myself. To me, I think a song is a punk song as long as it checks the musical boxes required of the genre. But to call the artist who made the song A punk themselves as an individual, they need to have the ethos. That's where I come down on it, at least.

But like I said, this debate has circled the drain for 50 years now. There's a joke I've seen a few times that says "there's nothing more punk than disagreeing about what punk is."

It can come more from the band's tradition than what they necessarily end up sounding like but either way I would say Linkin Park, Tool and Lamb of God are firmly metal. Nirvana certainly came from a punk underground, if their music came out in the late 70s no one would question it being called punk but they happened to be with a group of bands who got described otherwise, and had a few edgier elements that took them away from that. Sum41 are pop punk, I don't know if they plied their trade in basement clubs before going big but that is their sound.

Nirvana are absolutely punk School, Tourettes, Territorial Pissings are all quintessential punk songs. Kurt loved Black Flag, Wipers, Bad Brains, and Bauhaus and drew heavily on their influence.

Randy from Lamb of God is more into 80s hardcore/punk than metal but the band itself is groove metal

How music was before
When I bought a CD or a record, the music came with context. I'd read the liner notes front to back on the bus home, who produced it, who played on track 7, the thank-you list that mapped the artist's whole scene, the lyrics printed so you actually read them. The artwork was the size of your hands, not a thumbnail. You'd paid for it, so you gave it weeks instead of one distracted listen.

Now a track autoplays, I half-listen, and I move on before learning a single thing about who made it. I've had songs on repeat for months and couldn't name the producer or the year. The access is infinite and the attention is gone. Streaming is great at handing you the song and bad at handing you the artist.
I don't want to just romanticize scarcity, some of this is that I was a teenager with endless time, and Genius and Bandcamp do try to bring context back. But none of it feels load-bearing the way the object did.

Curious what everyone else thinks.

On the other hand the brevity of how music is presented on streaming encourages you to dig deeper in a different way, IMO. CDs and physical stuff is always better but I think it's great how streaming can open you up to finding smaller artists, obscurities, etc

I hear this sentiment often and never understood it. So what's stopping you?

You're the only one stopping yourself from listening to music like you used to. I was raised on having infinite access to music on slsk and what.cd as a teen, and I still listen to music like how you described at first almost exclusively. obsessively pouring over albums as a whole, reading lyrics, scouring places for interviews to understand it better, giving it my full attention. I almost never use streaming and the rare times when I do make sure autoplay is always off. It's as easy as a setting

Not trying to call you out or anything but just sounds to me like you enjoy the convenience of letting streaming autoplay out of your focus more than you enjoy what reward you get out of a more active and intentional listening. And that's fine, i think it's only a problem when people make that choice unconsciously.

I think the way music is presented and "consumed" is one thing. What's holding me back from really exploring new artists/albums is the fact that I don't have the time to explore like I used to and that I'm pretty set in what I like to listen to. There has to be a very good reason why I should listen to your music instead of the hundreds of classics that are dear to me from years of collecting. I was way more open for something new when I was younger. Now it's a choice between something that I know is great and look forward to enjoy again and new artists that more often than not don't stand a chance when compared with "the GOATS".

It’s definitely a different experience. I encourage you to take the necessary steps to bring that listening experience back with modern tools.

I’ve been using Qobuz instead of Spotify lately. It’s much more album-centric. The platform suggest albums and not songs. They have a huge description of the album written by someone who has actually heard it for many albums. There’s a built-in “magazine” with articles. I feel like I’m actually discovering artists again.

It also doesn’t auto-play stuff after the album is over. You have to go choose another thing to listen to.

Find a way to do something similar for yourself. In a limitless world you need to bring your own limits.

I've gotten a lot more intentional about listening and discovery in the past few years, even with streaming. I also grew up listening the same way you described, fell out of touch with music for a decade or so, and came back hard in the pandemic. I'm still an album listener. Streaming is an amazing tool for discovery, but the algorithm is... not.

The ability to look up just about any artist I hear about and press play has made my music taste get a whole lot weirder. I was 90s teenager who obsessively read music journalism and frustrated that I couldn't access so many of the influences that my favorite artists talked about in interviews unless I used my precious allowance money to take a chance on buying a CD just because it was on Kurt Cobain's list of favorite albums or whatever. Now I can fill in gaps (huh, have I really never sat down and listened to Leonard Cohen?) and chase down tangents (let's queue up Kurt's list!) and go down rabbit holes of microgenres (holy hell, apparently I love Japanese art punk?!). That would've blown my 15-year-old mind.

I tolerate autoplay only when I have music on in the background and I'm too busy to pick something else. The algorithm hasn't added a lot to my music tastes, but the access has. Curiosity is massively rewarded.

Are any of Arcade Fire’s albums significant?
My Apple Music served me up a bunch of Arcade Fire tonight and I started wondering about their body of work. I am looking for sincere opinions about this.

Are any of their albums significant? As in, did they move the needle culturally? Were they emotionally resonant? Did they hit a certain demographic at exactly the right time and make fans for life?

The Suburbs sounds to me like the sonic equivalent of Stranger Things. It hits the right notes but it’s more of a throwback to something significant than it is something of significance in and of itself. Their music just feels empty, in a way, even though it had all the trappings of being emotional.

I am wondering if I missed something.

Funeral, Neon Bible and Suburbs were pretty damn good… I’d say Arcade Fire was the most significant of the kinda indie rock revival of the 2000’s. I’ve never been too interested in much of their later albums and also agree listening back it was kind of ‘of it’s time’- I’d point out that while the whole nostalgia thing has been over done, these guys were kind of on the leading edge of that. Also Wake Up and Sprawl II are absolute masterpieces

Absolutely. Funeral was huge. The instrumentation was very unique at the time (at least it was for me). Maybe this is a Seinfeld situation where they don’t seem that special now but I would bet that any millennial/older gen z indie band with a violin or accordion will say that arcade fire was a huge influence.

Suburbs was just a really really good album that year (It got the Best Album Grammy award) and it came out at the perfect time. The stranger things comparison is brilliant.

Reflektor blended different genres and showed they were a truly inventive band and not just a one trick pony.

You gotta keep in mind that during this era of music Spotify didn’t exist. There were a lot less bands people were being exposed to. Arcade Fire was a blue chip indie band that were always in rotation for cool college kids. I’ve actually never heard anyone say they didn’t like them until I asked a 24 year old friend of mine what he thought of them. “They’re ok” was a laughable response but I guess without context I could see how nowadays they don’t seem like the pioneers that they were.

Funeral is one of the best albums of the 21st century, and it's one of the albums that really kicked off a mid 2000s - early 2010s wave of mainstream interest in indie rock. The sound is unpolished yet instrumentally lush, and the songs are lyrically earnest but dramatically arranged. It basically created a market niche bridging humble indie songwriter sensibilities to sing-along arena anthems. Many other artists since then have tried to thread that needle into mainstream fame, but few have managed to sound as authentic while doing it. Songs like "Wake Up" are modern classics, and aside from being widely reproduced in TV and movies, they can still get complete strangers sing along together when they come on. That's a cultural footprint.

Neon Bible is arguably just as good of an album, if not better. It sounds a lot more confident and mature and takes much bigger sonic risks that pay off. But culturally it's been overshadowed by the bigger chart hits and prime mover advantage from Funeral. You can listen to both albums back to back as basically the same era in the band's history.

I'd say their first three albums did have an impact. The Suburbs did win Album of the Year at the Grammys, which I recall being a surprise. It's their first one, though, that really hit. Funeral came along at a perfect time as Millennials were hitting adulthood. It captured their experience of growing up and gave voice to their coming of age struggles. It was also an excellent album.

For me, it took a minute to get into them. I thought they were too serious and Win Butler was off-putting even back then. However, when it did click for me, I found their music to be sublime.

They were also an excellent live band. Being a 7-piece band (maybe bigger), there was an epic quality to their performances.

Im late to the conversation so don’t know if you’ll see this but there’s a lot to this discussion I didn’t see anyone mention.

David Bowie promoted arcade fire as the next big thing.

The song “wake up” from funeral was a bonafide crossover radio, film and television hit. It was the anthem used to market the “where the wild things are” movie.

They were all the rage with taste makers of their time: pitchfork and urban outfitters promoted them.

They were one of if not the major contributor to the indie folk wave that followed them (fleet foxes, bon iver, lumineers, Mumford and sons, hozier).

They were famous for having a huge lineup of musicians on stage, and I personally saw many indie bands copy this along with their indie folk inspirations.

We all age. But, do artists and bands playing live age?
Bottom line, we all age.

Sports athletes, obviously, they won't be as good older compared to in their prime. Any athlete in their early to late 30's and beyond won't be as peak as they were in the start of their career to mid-20's.

So, do musicians and bands performing live... sound?!.. different? Obviously the Rolling Stones of today won't sound like 60's-70's Stones. That's a ton of years difference. I get that. I get that solo vocalists sound different at different stages. Young Frank Sinatra compared to late Sinatra. It's different.

But I saw Dave Matthews Band recently. They were great, sounded fine to me for the most part. But I sure would have loved to see them in the late 90's, 2000's. Playing a guitar, drums, bass in 1990 will sound the same as today. Vocals can be another story. But still, does the music performed live seem to age in some way?

The biggest difference is vocals. The real reason we'll never see a Led Zeppelin reunion is that Robert Plant simply can't wail like that anymore.

Side note though, huge respect to Plant for leaning into it and singing his own stuff in a style that suits how his voice has aged, and doing so apparently without ego. His folk band's NPR Tiny Desk video is well worth a watch.

There's a handful of acts I've seen more or less every year for up to 26 years now in DMB's case, few others 15-20 years, bunch of others 10ish years, etc. Plus I listen to a ton of live recordings for all sorts of acts.

The most glaringly obvious ones are 1) male vocalists in particular tend to get noticeably deeper voices as they age and 2) the music tends to be played at gradually slower speeds over the years.

2 examples I've seen recently where this topic occurred to me (while listening):

I saw Iggy Pop on his tour last year. His voice is noticably weaker and his movements on stage were less... dramatic. Still a fantastic live show and a great performer, but far removed from the primal screams, crowd surfing and self-harm performances of past decades.

I watched The Cure on the Primavera festival live stream, and was struck by how well Robert Smith's voice has held up, the dood can still belt out the hits, as well as, or perhaps even better than in previous decades.

Another aspect to this conversation is the band members (i.e. not just vocalists), who are often not the same as on original recordings or prime-era performances, and the technology used by the band, which can certainly affect the sound (i.e. digital keyboards vs. synths, or analog instrumentation vs. samples).

Sometimes a band member leaves, and the loss of their characteristic sound means a band changes direction entirely (i.e. Mick Ronson leaving Bowie's band, or Ozzy's band after Randy Rhodes death).

Robert Smith is only in his 60s. I think one of the things that make the Cure’s late career seem so unlikely is that its hard to reconcile that they were very, very young when they made their first couple of albums, wise beyond their years.

To your other point, the rest of the Cure have kept up with the times in terms of sound design, they haven’t retreated into reliving a dated sound on their new songs.

Saw Tori Amos in the 90s and very recently on her current tour. Of course the energy is different. Song choices are a factor, vocal range and power is….the thing is though: If an artist has the self awareness of what getting older entails and the talent to pull it off, then aging is not a factor that is detrimental. 90s Tori Amos was an explosion of virtuosity and gargantuan talent with so much energy it was almost painful to watch, her current vibe is deeper, darker, swampier, a bit more laid back but no less fascinating.

But it is evident that it takes a lot of reflection and skill to not just be a shadow of oneself but make aging an actual strength - which makes „successfully aging artists“ good teachers.

Musicians seem capable of making it further into old age without losing their skill. Some performers will develop arthritis that might limit how often or how long they can play, but we often see people reaching advanced ages and still being able to play complicated pieces with excellent proficiency.

But a few things that do change are voices and styles. Our voices drop as we age, and they can even wear out to the point where we struggle to hit notes cleanly. Elton John famously lost his falsetto voice, which was a big part of his early music.

I’ve also noticed that musicians will often become interested in new styles of music as they age, and sometimes this will influence the way they play music. Someone like Eric Clapton who performed Layla as a loud, energetic blues rock song now plays it more like a jazz standard on an acoustic guitar.

So it’s not always about a musician losing their abilities. Aging could just mean that the musician doesn’t want to play their music in the same way anymore.

You hit on it but maybe didn’t notice — “I sure would have loved to see them in the late 90’s, 2000’s” isn’t really a statement about how the band sounds. It’s about you. You’re not missing 1999 DMB, you’re missing whoever you were in 1999 listening to them.

The guitar/bass/drums part you already answered yourself. A G chord in 1990 sounds like a G chord now. What “ages” is rarely the instrument. It’s the voice, sure, but mostly it’s the room you’re standing in and the person standing in it.

I’ve been seeing live shows for 38 years and the ones that hit hardest weren’t the technically best performances. They were the ones tangled up with where my life was at the time. Saw Springsteen across three different decades — the playing got tighter if anything, but the version of me showing up changed every time. That’s the variable nobody controls for.

So when you ask “does live music age” — whose aging are we actually measuring?

Depending on the genre, tempos tend to slow as band members age. This is especially true for more technically complex music requiring virtuoso players. Yes cannot play "Gates of Delirium" at 200bpm anymore, not with its members approaching eighty. They've slowed it down a good 20-30bpm on recent live tours. When Nick Mason toured early Pink Floyd songs a few years ago, he didn't play "Set the Controls for the Heart of the Sun" with anywhere near the vigor as he did fifty odd years ago in Pompeii (which, if you've seen the concert video, it pretty damn energetic).

Hell, I saw Ben Folds with the Boston Pops last week and even at 59, he struggled with his playing and absolutely could not sing the highs like he did in 1998. (It was still a great show, however!)

So yes, to your question - time comes for all of us, even the gods of the stage.

Even if you manage to keep your body in a good shape, father Time still takes something off you.

Russell Mael from Sparks is an example of it. His vocals still hold up well in his advanced years, but he can't do some stuff like he used to. I guess we're never gonna hear Never Turn Your Back On Mother Earth live again.

But the sheer will to go on tour, when you're approaching freakin' 80,is commendable

What caused the development of popular music between 1945 and 1956 to stall?
For anyone who doesn't know, this is roughly the time period between the end of World War II with the demise of Big Band Swing and the commercial breakthrough of Rock and Roll.

It seems that around the midst of America's entry into World War 2, the popularity of Swing music and Jazz more broadly began to decline, and after the end of the war, the decline only accelerated. Jazz continued to develop, but Glenn Miller had died by then, Artie Shaw had retired from the limelight and the likes of Benny Goodman, Count Basie and Duke Ellington, who continued to evolve their style were exiled from the mainstream and were forced to lead much smaller groups than the orchestras of their glory days, while newer musicians like Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie and Miles Davis made their homes in small clubs rather than the ballrooms of the 1920s and 30s.

Mainstream music on the other hand seemed to slow down, both figuratively and literally. I'd personally characterize a lot of this music as the dreary stuff that would put you to sleep, while many of the most popular singers like Perry Como, Doris Day, Nat King Cole and Frank Sinatra are fairly well regarded today, a lot of the other popular music at the time tended to be either slower renditions of GAS songs with a backing orchestra playing very watered down Big Band music, or they were ridiculous novelty songs that really aren't regarded with much merit. Infamous examples include the Shrimp Boats, Come On-A My House, Mambo Italiano, How Much Is That Doggie and others.

Rhythm and Blues on the other hand, and Country to a lesser extent, maintained the high energy of Swing, had a heavier, more danceable beat and was generally made with more sincerity and passion than what was being played on mainstream radio, it had evolved from the Jump Blues that Count Basie and Louis Jordan had popularized in the early 40s with a touch of Gospel. By 1955, R&B had developed into Doo-Wop and Proto-Soul, but it also had been fused with Country to give us early hits like Rock Around The Clock, That's All Right and Maybellene, which heralded the arrival of Rock & Roll. This gained massive popularity among young people at the time and essentially changed pop music permanently.

So what do you think it was that caused Mainstream Pop before Rock and Roll to lose it's energy and appeal? Was it the booming economy and optimism that lead to Pop music at the time to lose it's sincerity and artistic merit, or did the music industry just do whatever was financially more viable?

The decline of big bands began during the 1942–1944 musicians' strike. Because instrumentalists were banned from recording, record labels relied on vocalists, who were not in the union, to keep producing music. This launched singers like Frank Sinatra to solo superstardom.

Then, following World War II, rising travel costs, wartime rationing, and the economic difficulty of keeping large ensembles together cemented the decline of big bands. Singers like Frank Sinatra, Nat King Cole, Dean Martin, Doris Day, and Perry Como dominated the pop charts, backed by lush stringed instruments rather than jazz big bands.

Edit: In addition, the transition of radio stations to 7-inch 45 RPM singles by 1954 radically transformed the music industry. It eliminated the fragile, bulky 78, allowing stations to build deep playlists, manage heavy rotation, and easily queue up records. This portability and convenience fueled the explosion of Top 40 radio and youth-driven Rock 'n' Roll. Most radio stations just tossed their 78s, thus instantly making many of the recordings from before 1954 obscure and hard to find.

This is an absolutely bonkers take considering the development of Bop during that exact post-war period, which you mention but then sort of dismiss out of hand? But yeah, that's probably the most important (and energetic!) development in the history of jazz to that point. Yes, the clubs they were playing in were smaller as jazz's more middle road white mass audience didn't couldn't come to terms with the leap forward, but history has proven the musicians right in that the moment was at least as important as rock n roll (frankly more so as the years go on).

When talking about music, the period from 1900 up to 2000, follows really really close the development of the instruments and audio equipment. 

After 2000, when everything was sampled inside the computer and anyone could do anything, things started to follow multiple trends and sound derivative.

However, starting with 1900, lots of fresh hardware affected the sound, how music was made, and what music was made, eventually: drum kits (sizes, material and tuning), recording techniques (allowed for overdubbing), electric guitar, the electric keyboards, amp overdrive, full-on distortion, first synthesizers and the list go on.

The hardware was the tick and the resulting music was the tock for a good 100 years.

I believe that the war may have been directed the electronics research into military-related fields, so we have a bit of a gap, but as soon as the war ended, things got back to normal. 

The Moog synths are famously made with electronic components used by the military, just to have a long serviceable life (the components for military equipment are usually produced for decades).

TV allowed people to enjoy entertainment programming at home. Before that, especially in cities where people were living in apartments with family members or roommates, it was common to go out to catch a movie, go dancing in a music, or attend some event with live musical entertainment.

The shift was largely driven by economics. Big bands are expensive, with twelve, or even 16 mouths to feed. A typical Rock-n-Roll combo has only four members. If fewer people are venturing out to live music venues, promoters can’t afford to book big bands, and band leaders have to let some musicians go. And electronic amplification meant one guitar and one bass could fill a ballroom with big sound that previously required a big horn section and more. This spelled the end of the big band era, and the end of new music for those large orchestras. Jazz moved to smaller clubs, where are three or four piece acoustic combo were loud enough to be heard, and small enough to get paid.

I think it was just more fragmented where the Rock N Roll explosion really consolidated youth culture. You had Bill Monroe thriving enough to travel with a full sized baseball team to play the local teams post show and rock n roll basically killed young audiences for bluegrass. Bebop jazz: John Coltrane. miles Davis’ Kinda Blue. Texas swing such as Bob Wills. Hank Williams and Earnest Tubb. Chicago/electric/City Blues (“boy, don’t you know Muddy Waters invented electricity)”

Your favorite musicians’ favorite musicians
As a speculative exercise today I was trying to put together a list of 10 albums that I consider favorites, that I feel represent my tastes well, and that aren‘t just the usual suspects praised constantly by everyone. (The idea was that these would be the choices I’d pick if were asked to do something like The Quietus’ favorite records series, even though there’s no reason I would ever be picked for something like that; vain, I know!)

I won‘t share my list here, but it struck me as I was narrowing down my choices how many artists that I probably would've picked five or ten years ago I was leaving off, and also how many of the artists I *was* picking were introduced to me through those earlier favorites, or at least were important influences on them. I left off Jeff Buckley but included Nina Simone and Leonard Cohen. I left off my teenage favorite Thrice but included Slowdive, a band I originally learned about through an interview with Thrice’s Dustin Kensrue. Almost everything on the list could be traced in this way to something else that used to be a cherished favorite of mine, until I started to get more interested in what had led there, which in turn gave me a new, even more cherished favorite.

I feel like this is a pretty typical trajectory for dedicated music fans, who are always looking to discover previously-unfamiliar things and to understand the precedents and lineages for the music they like. On the other hand, it seems a lot of people do just kind of settle on their favorites in their teens or twenties and don’t really branch out from there. And of course some “dedicated music fans“ are probably more interested in the present than the past, and are eager to discover new artists and sounds rather than dwelling on the old.

I guess if I were to shape all this into some discussion questions they would be: How much of your listening life do you spend investigating your favorites‘ favorites? How much of the music you love now did you find because it was an influence on someone else? And how many old favorites have become overshadowed for you by the things that inspired them?

To try and put it into words, I've recently come to realize that there's this undercurrent of looking for source. Not in a competitive, who did it first sorta way, but like pulling a long root out of the garden dirt. I've been playing instruments since middle school and have enjoyed simply finding context across the decades. (And sure, some if it is to try and find a niche that hasn't been covered).

Finding my favorites-favorites is a way to find more of what I like, but categorizing it in some historical sense is just as fascinating. To see how a genre was formed, to see a different class make it popular, to see a different class reshape it. It's neat how organic it can be without directly ripping something off.

Honestly, it's kind of overwhelming, but it makes sense coming from an artist with a diverse palette who worked at a record store for a while.

I kind of prefer more organized lists, or Panda Bear's own admission that 'everything [he] wanted to be in a band he learned from Black Dice'. That kind of fellow-reverence really helps guide a listener, more than an endless list.

Even Jeff Buckley has a nice little thing to say about Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan on his Live at Sin-é record. Dijon's song "Talk Down" uses band/musician names almost as a theme for that song. Alan Palomo's recent "Stay at Home DJ" also lists off bunches of musicians, as does Daft Punk's (imo less successful) "Teachers".

Has having unlimited access to music made us more open-minded listeners?
Years ago, most people were limited by what was on the radio, in local record stores, or whatever their friends were listening to. Now you can explore almost any genre, artist, or scene in seconds.

Because of that, I'd expect people to have broader tastes than ever before.

But sometimes it feels like the opposite happened. Instead of exploring more, a lot of us end up staying in our own lanes while algorithms keep feeding us similar music.

Do you think listeners today are actually more open-minded than previous generations, or do we just have more options available to us?

Access didn’t make us more open. It made the cost of choosing higher. Time per day is the same as it always was, but the amount of music in front of you went from a few hundred records at the local store to basically infinite. When the pile gets that big, picking becomes work, and most people don’t have the energy to do that work every day after everything else.

So the instinct kicks in: listen to nothing, or listen to what you already know. Not because people got lazy or closed-minded, just because the brain protects itself from decision overload. The radio era was limiting, but limits also did the filtering for you. Now you’re the filter, all the time, and that’s exhausting.

And it gets worse, not better. The more music there is, the heavier the choosing gets, the more people retreat into the familiar. More options doesn’t equal more listening. It mostly equals more standing in front of the open fridge with the door open.

I can't remember whether it was covered in the book Ripped by Greg Kot or whether I read it in another book about the time period around the rise of internet sharing and such in the 90's early 2000s(Could have been a drive-by topic in Such Great Heights by Chris DeVille), but the claim made was absolutely--but I think it's an earlier phenomenon than your question sort of places it, since the Q seems framed from a modern streaming standpoint. It actually talks about college radio as a phenomenon that started the de-genre-ing of music even though in the early years there was a 'sound' to those stations before they became the interesting left of the dial melting pots that we talk about them as now. It goes on to talk then about how the Blog Era letting creators and curators who were previously ignored by the radio for either being too weird or seen as umarketable for other reasons, becoming succesful not only by the lower cost of self distribution on the internet but also by the boost from genre-agnostic curators and mixtape makers who ran blogs or put out compilations.

But yeah, since traditional radio stations all had genre based identities, once there were other good ways to step outside of that system, people embraced it. I am also basing this off of being a teenager in the blog era and how there was suddenly a shift from people saying "oh I listen to (genre)" towards "oh I listen to everything but (genre)" because the deluge of hard-to-categorize music had gotten so overwhelming. In the very early 'blog era' I remember a whole list of basically 'indie listener acceptable' electronic music that had been sort of adopted into the scene. Even now when you look up "indie sleaze" you will find a huge mix of what most people who consider indie as a genre sound and certain kinds of electronic music. Indie kids started listening to rap music more at this time too and from there it all snowballed. Maybe the pendelum has swung the other direction in the decades since but at the advent of wider genre-agnostic listening, it was embraced and people gladly broadened their horizons to be the first to know the next best thing.

it amazes me how many people listen to music that challenges them and never give it another try instead of attempting to get into it and listen multiple times until it eventually clicks

Because for most people music isn't a hobby and it isn't something they obsess over. They aren't trying to find new textures, new artists, something experimental that takes time to fully digest. They just want something that feels good that they can vibe to. There's nothing wrong with that, but that's the way most people digest music.

Folks on r/books have a pretty consistent take on a similar and frequently asked set of questions—do you continue reading a book you’re not enjoying? If not, how long do you give it? How do you know if you’ve given it a fair shot? Should you persist in reading classics or avant-garde or otherwise challenging books that you don’t like? The consensus over there, with some detractors, is that life is short and you’ll never read all the books you want to read so why punish yourself by finishing something because other people like it? What Makes a Song Endure Over Others?
What makes one song released in a time period endure more so than other songs from that same time period? What factors go in? Does it have to immediately grab you with some irreplaceable hook or something? This is something I wonder, how certain songs continue to endure while others tend to fade out.

A great example I think would be Bon Jovi's Livin on a Prayer. Whatever you think of that song or Bon Jovi, we can't deny it has endured tremendously. I feel like every generation will know or at least heard that song once in their lives. When I am on my deathbed I feel like I will still now every line to that song. But why then do we remember that song whereas other hit songs from that time have faded from public memory. Why has this song endured when say Sara by Starship, Shakedown by Bob Seger, and Always by Atlantic Star haven't?

To a larger extent than most people will admit, the answer is luck - though a level of competence and (in the popular music world) simplicity also count. Many of the "irreplaceable hooks" are actually nothing new in a fundamental sense, but happened to be produced better, or catch some spirit of popularity which carried over into enduring fame. To speak of Bon Jovi in particular, it's catchy, there's no doubt, but to be honest I don't think people will remember it much beyond Bon Jovi's own lifetime. I listened to "Sara" by Starship prompted by you as well. And it's catchy too, but it's not in the current fashion which aims to be "heavier" and it's over 5 minutes when it has musical material for maybe 3 minutes, if that. Forty years may seem like a long time if you've never listened much to Chopin or Bach or Perotin, but fashions not only change but much bigger changes come after the generation that were alive at the time of the artist begins to die off. I wouldn't place bets on anything of Bon Jovi's being widely known in another 80 years. There are thousands of pretty good songs that are almost completely forgotten 30 years later - and some of them are even to some extent more "original" than the more famous ones. So luck has a lot to do with it.

Is Nirvana a far less weird band than the Red Hot Chili Peppers?
Nirvana are a more respected band in the music community and both have roughly the same level of commercial success. Nirvana is primarily straightforward metallic punk rock, built on loud-quiet song structures, murky guitar riffs, and strong pop melodies. RHCP on the other hand have mixed more styles like funk, punk, jazz, rap, metal, psychedelic rock, orchestral pop, and jam-band influences, which makes me think of them as almost like a less heavy version of Faith No More. Most would also agree that Flea and John Frusciante are more adventurous players than Krist and Kurt. However, one could argue that Nirvana never really got a chance to show off their experimental side since the band ended so early.

How do you define "weird"?

Musicality-wise, they're on different spectrums but also because they have different goals as artists. Nirvana aimed to be a counterculture to the prevailing hair metal/glam rock bands. Thus, they showed up in everyday clothes, with some from thrift shops.

Kurt did like to play the guitar but didn't actively seek to learn theory. Despite that, his love for the instrument made him and the rest of the band able to create a lot of banger songs that are musically simple but have emotions.

On the other hand, Flea was more into playing fast and intense slap and pop until Frusciante came into the band, who had a lot of ideas grounded on music theory. Eventually, Flea would have his own academic pursuit, but overall, almost every music they wrote are under the umbrella of funk and rock.

Flea and Frusciante both influenced me more as a musician (in bass and guitar) since I'm a nerd who likes to know stuff. However, I also acknowledge the side of music that is about expressing yourself, your ideas from musical to political, and about emotions. In the latter side, Nirvana has influenced me more since not only do I genuinely like the intensity of their music, but they also actively voiced out their hate against racism, misogyny and homophobia. Maybe it's also part of the reason why Nirvana is respected.

I really like the atmosphere of Siouxsie & the Banshees’ early days.
Always been a fan of the band, but for the past month-ish or so, I’ve really enjoyed their early era - particularly their 1978 debut album “The Scream”. There are so many elements that I love about that part of the band’s career.

I’ll start with John McKay (who was the guitarist for the Banshees from 1977 to 1979). I think he’s an incredible and hugely underrated guitarist…..I’m not a musician, nor do I know much about music theory, so I’m simply going by ear here. But to me, the things that he did on the guitar were incredible. I’m impressed with the uniqueness of his style….I got this imagery of spikes & barbed wire whenever I’m focusing on his work. John McKay’s guitar parts had this angular & jagged element…..like you’re getting chopped up by a really sharp blade. And when the Banshees covered the Beatles’ “Helter Skelter”….OMG! The intro literally sounds like a precursor to Sonic Youth or even Big Black. I think that unusual chords were involved too….the stuff that John McKay did can’t be replicated by anybody in my opinion.

And I dig the more uncompromising mood. I feel that something like Juju is more colorful & psychedelic, whereas The Scream has more of an edge to it. You can really hear the punk background of the Banshees….the riffing & the faster parts are very similar to the Sex Pistols & the Buzzcocks. And The Scream has this harshness & a tougher, more stark feel that I really like….things like the dissonance of “Pure”, the last minute or so of “Suburban Relapse”….it felt so raw to me. Felt like I was in a horror film!

I dig other elements of the Banshees’ early days too, like the drumming that reminded me of CAN & Moe Tucker’s work for the Velvet Underground. And the supple basslines & Siouxsie Sioux’s Bowie-ish vocals. I like to compare The Scream with other post-punk groups like Wire, Magazine & Gang of Four. Great stuff overall!

Do you discover music through genres or through feelings/atmospheres?
Today I was thinking about how differently people navigate music:

Some people seem to explore almost entirely through genres: doom metal - sludge - funeral doom - drone, etc;

or house - deep house - minimal - ambient techno

Others seem to navigate music more through atmosphere or emotional texture: “music for rainy nights,” “music that feels like winter,” “music that sounds ancient,” and so on.

I’m wondering whether genre labels shape the way we hear music more than we realize?

Sometimes a genre tag helps me understand context and lineage. Other times it feels limiting, like it tells me what I’m supposed to hear before I even press play.

And I’m curious how do you usually discover music?

- Through genres?

- Through moods/atmospheres?

- Through scenes/labels?

- Algorithms?

- Other people?

And how important do you think genre labels actually are to the way we perceive music?

What was it like hearing Led Zeppelin for the first time in 1969?
I'm 29(M) and have always loved Led Zeppelin. Grew up listening to them and they are probably my favorite rock band outside of Pink Floyd.

I recently watched the documentary Becoming Led Zeppelin on Netflix and the big takeaway I had from it was Jimmy Page saying that he fought so hard for creative control from the beginning and there would be no singles because the albums were meant to be listened top to bottom.

So, yesterday I listened to their entire discography top to bottom in order (I usually listen to music album top to bottom) and it's hard to describe but after watching the documentary and learning what the band intended with their music, the way they left space for each other to shine on each song, it just was a truly wonderful experience and I felt like I was rediscovering Led Zeppelin all over again.

To my question (which is actually more than one)...What was it like when they first released their album? What did you feel when you first heard it? What was it like when they followed up each album with another classic? Lastly, what's your favorite album and why?

Would love to hear stories and experiences from that time!
